venue: Hard Rock Stadium, Miami Gardens, USA
h2h_summary: Brazil dominate the all-time record, winning 4 of 5 meetings (all at World Cups) with Scotland's only positive result a 0-0 draw in 1974; Scotland have never beaten Brazil.

notable_results: 0-0 draw - FIFA World Cup Group Stage - 1974
1-4 loss (David Narey goal) - FIFA World Cup Group Stage - 1982
1-2 loss (Collins pen, Boyd OG) - FIFA World Cup Group Stage - 1998

team_style_home: Steve Clarke's Scotland play a disciplined 4-2-3-1 with a compact, hard-working midfield block, relying on defensive organisation to frustrate opponents and quick transitions through McTominay and wide runners.
team_style_away: Carlo Ancelotti's Brazil combine Brazilian attacking flair with Italian defensive structure in a 4-2-3-1 / 4-3-3 hybrid, using wide forwards Vinicius and Raphinha to exploit space on the break while Guimaraes anchors midfield.
wc_history_home: Scotland have appeared at eight World Cups (1974-1998, now 2026) but have never progressed past the group stage, twice going out on goal difference despite remaining unbeaten.
wc_history_away: Brazil are the most successful nation in World Cup history with five titles (1958, 1962, 1970, 1994, 2002) and are the only team to have participated in every tournament.
group_context: Group C contains five-time world champions Brazil, 2022 semi-finalists Morocco, Scotland (returning after a 28-year absence), and debutant-returnee Haiti; Brazil and Morocco are heavy favourites to advance, leaving Scotland and Haiti fighting for any remaining spot via the expanded third-place berths.
strengths_home: Defensive organisation and hard-to-beat structure under Clarke
Scott McTominay's box-crashing goals and dynamic midfield energy
Tournament-hardened leadership through Robertson and McGinn
strengths_away: World-class attacking duo in Vinicius Junior and Raphinha
Tactical adaptability and man-management under Carlo Ancelotti
Depth across all positions with Brazil's strongest squad in years
